profile.name: Ben Rich
profile.born: June 18, 1925 - Baku, Azerbaijan (raised in Los Angeles, California)
roles: Lead Engineer - U-2 Reconnaissance Aircraft Program
Program Director - F-117 Nighthawk Stealth Fighter
profile.summary: Ben Rich was the second director of Lockheed's Skunk Works advanced development division and the principal engineer behind the F-117 Nighthawk, the world's first operational stealth aircraft. Near the end of his life, he made a series of statements - in private correspondence and at a 1993 UCLA alumni event - suggesting that classified U.S. programs possessed propulsion technology capable of interstellar travel. These claims, delivered by one of the most credentialed aerospace engineers in American history, remain among the most debated secondhand testimonies in UAP research.
early_career: Joined Lockheed's Skunk Works division in Burbank, California in 1950 under founder Kelly Johnson
Served as lead thermodynamic engineer on the U-2 high-altitude reconnaissance aircraft (1955)
Led propulsion and thermal engineering for the A-12/SR-71 Blackbird program (1959-1968)
Named deputy director of Skunk Works by Kelly Johnson in 1965

key_events.event: Joined Lockheed's Skunk Works division after graduating from UCLA, beginning a four-decade career in classified aerospace development under founder Kelly Johnson.

Served as lead thermodynamic engineer on the U-2 spy plane, one of the CIA's most consequential Cold War intelligence platforms, solving critical heat rejection problems that made sustained high-altitude flight possible.
Led propulsion and thermal systems engineering for the SR-71 Blackbird, the fastest air-breathing manned aircraft ever flown, capable of Mach 3.2 at 85,000 feet.
Succeeded Kelly Johnson as director of Lockheed Skunk Works, inheriting stewardship of the nation's most sensitive black aerospace programs.

The F-117 Nighthawk - the world's first operational stealth aircraft - completed its maiden flight under Rich's program direction, validating the faceted low-observable design approach Rich had championed.

Wrote a letter to researcher Jan Harzan stating: 'I am a believer in both categories. I feel everything is possible. Many of our manmade UFOs are unfunded opportunities.' The letter is the only known written primary source from Rich on UAP-related claims.
Retired from Lockheed after 41 years and began work on his memoir with co-author Leo Janos.

Delivered a presentation at a UCLA School of Engineering alumni dinner. Attendees including Jan Harzan (later MUFON director) reported Rich stated: 'We now have the technology to take ET home. No it won't take someone's lifetime to do it. There's an error in the equations. We now know what it is.' No recording of the speech exists.
Published 'Skunk Works: A Personal Memoir of My Years at Lockheed' with Leo Janos. The memoir covers the history of Skunk Works through the F-117 but contains no reference to UAP-related claims.
Died of prostate cancer in Los Angeles at age 69. His alleged statements about classified propulsion technology became the subject of sustained researcher scrutiny in the years that followed.

claims.claim: Allegedly stated at a 1993 UCLA alumni engineering dinner that classified U.S. programs possessed propulsion technology capable of interstellar travel: 'We now have the technology to take ET home. There's an error in the equations. We now know what it is.'
claims.notes: No audio or video recording exists. The account originates primarily from Jan Harzan, who attended the event and became MUFON director. Multiple other attendees have separately corroborated Harzan's account, but the statement cannot be independently confirmed through any contemporaneous documentation.

claims.claim: In a 1986 letter, Rich wrote: 'I am a believer in both categories [manmade and extraterrestrial UFOs]. I feel everything is possible. Many of our manmade UFOs are unfunded opportunities.'
claims.notes: The letter is a documented primary source reproduced in multiple published accounts. It is one of the few written records in which a senior U.S. aerospace executive acknowledged both the existence of classified manmade unconventional craft programs and a personal belief in extraterrestrial craft.

claims.claim: Rich allegedly wrote to aerospace illustrator John Andrews: 'Yes, I'm a believer in both categories. There are two types of UFOs - the ones we build, and ones they build.' He added that the U.S. had the means to 'take ET home' using black program technology.
claims.notes: The Andrews correspondence is reproduced in multiple UAP publications including Tom Keller's 2010 MUFON article. Chain-of-custody authentication has not been independently established. Andrews died in 1999; the letters were disclosed posthumously by his family.

credibility.assessment: Rich's professional credentials are impeccable - 41 years directing the most sensitive U.S. black aerospace programs places him in a tiny category of individuals with genuine firsthand knowledge of classified technology. The limiting factor is that his key statements are unrecorded and secondhand, delivered in private settings with no contemporaneous documentation. The 1986 letter provides a rare written primary source but stops well short of explicit claims about recovered craft or extraterrestrial technology.
supporting: 41-year career as director and deputy director of Lockheed Skunk Works - the apex of U.S. classified aerospace development
The 1986 Harzan letter is a documented written primary source in which Rich explicitly references manmade unconventional aircraft and expresses belief in extraterrestrial craft
Multiple independent attendees at the 1993 UCLA event corroborate Harzan's account of the speech
Rich had no apparent incentive to fabricate - he was near death from cancer and his professional legacy was already secured
His access to the deepest levels of U.S. black programs gives any statements he made extraordinary weight compared to secondhand witnesses
contradicting: No audio, video, or contemporaneous written record of the 1993 UCLA speech exists
The most explosive quotes originate from a single primary witness (Jan Harzan) at a private dinner - not a public setting
Rich may have been speaking metaphorically about classified stealth and propulsion advances rather than literal ET-derived technology
His memoir 'Skunk Works' (1994), written at the same time as these alleged statements, contains zero UAP references - suggesting he did not consider this on-record
Proximity to death from cancer may have affected the context, intent, or later recollection of any informal statements

associated_people.name: Col. John Alexander
associated_people.role: U.S. Army non-lethal weapons director; UAP researcher
associated_people.relationship: Alexander was among the UAP researchers Rich reportedly corresponded with in the years before his death. Alexander's own research network intersected with multiple Skunk Works-connected figures.

associated_people.name: Mark McCandlish
associated_people.role: USAF aerospace illustrator; Alien Reproduction Vehicle witness
associated_people.relationship: McCandlish's 1988 Norton Air Force Base testimony about three craft of different sizes displayed in a hangar directly implicates the organizational structure Rich directed at Skunk Works. McCandlish's ARV diagrams are widely circulated in connection with black program reverse-engineering claims.

associated_people.name: Nick Cook
associated_people.role: Jane's Defence Weekly aviation editor; author - The Hunt for Zero Point
associated_people.relationship: Cook's decade-long investigation into classified antigravity research, published in 2001, examined the same Skunk Works-era black programs Rich directed and drew on many of the same institutional sources.

disclosures.title: Letter to Jan Harzan (1986)
disclosures.notes: Written letter to researcher Jan Harzan stating belief in both manmade and extraterrestrial UFOs and referencing 'unfunded opportunities' in classified aerospace. Widely reproduced in UAP research literature and considered the primary written primary source for Rich's claims.

disclosures.title: UCLA School of Engineering Alumni Dinner (1993)
disclosures.notes: Alumni dinner presentation at which attendees reported Rich stated classified technology existed for interstellar travel. No recording exists; account primarily attributed to Jan Harzan and corroborated by other attendees.

disclosures.title: Skunk Works: A Personal Memoir of My Years at Lockheed
disclosures.notes: Rich's published memoir co-authored with Leo Janos. Covers Skunk Works history from Kelly Johnson through the F-117. Does not address UAP-related claims. Considered the authoritative public record of Skunk Works' documented history.

sources.title: Skunk Works: A Personal Memoir of My Years at Lockheed - Ben Rich and Leo Janos (1994)
sources.notes: Rich's own memoir; primary biographical and career source

sources.title: Tom Keller - 'Ben Rich and the Secret of Stealth' - MUFON Journal (2010)
sources.notes: First systematic compilation of the UCLA speech claims; documents Jan Harzan's eyewitness account and the John Andrews correspondence

sources.title: Ben Rich Letters - UFO Evidence compilation
sources.notes: Compiles the known Rich correspondence including the 1986 Harzan letter and the Andrews letters

sources.title: Jan Harzan interview - MUFON (2014)
sources.notes: Harzan's detailed account of the 1993 UCLA speech and his correspondence with Rich

career_connections.relationship: Cook's investigation into classified antigravity research examined the same Skunk Works black programs Rich directed.
Alexander was among the UAP researchers Rich reportedly corresponded with before his death.
McCandlish's 1988 testimony about three craft displayed at Norton AFB directly implicates the Skunk Works programs Rich directed.
